(relationships between topographic and morphologic struc- tures) is controlled by various processes such as tectonics, climate, erosion and sea-level change ( Keller and Pinter, 1996; Montgomery et al., 2001; Burbank and Anderson, 2012 ). These relationships help to generate the tectonic his- tory of the region. In this context, analysis of morphomet- ric indices has been conventionally employed to explore the interaction between the landform geometry and tectonic deformation rates ( Centamore et al., 1996; Raj, 2012 ), which includes the study of the fluvial landforms such as drainage anomaly and analysis of the river basins ( Gailleton et al., 2019 ). Drainage quantification is an essen- tial method for assessing the basin morphology and the impact of geology (lithology and tectonics) and climate in terrain evolution of a region ( Merritts et al., 1994; Keller and Pinter, 1996; Montgomery et al., 2001; Pavano et al., 2018, 2019; Moumeni et al., 2024 ). Hence, the past terrain deformation can easily be understood by evaluating these variables through morphometric analysis of an area. SW Cameroon Atlantic Coast represented a part of the Central-African surface and dominated by the Mount Cameroon volcano, and displays a complex geological and geomorphological setting ( Fig. 1 ; Olivry, 1986; Boulvert, 1996; Nsangou Ngapna et al., 2019, 2020, 2023; Balla Ateba et al., 2021, 2023 ). It comprises the Archean Congo shield, the Paleoproterozoic Nyong and the Meso- Neoproterozoic North Equatorial orogenic belts ( Fig. 1 c– d; Maurizot et al., 1986; Feybesse et al., 1998 ), and is also covered by the Cretaceous Douala/Kribi-Campo and the Rio-Del-Rey basins separated by the Cenozoic Cameroon Volcanic Line (CVL, Fig. 1 b, d and e; Regnoult, 1986; Nguene et al., 1992; De´ruelle et al., 2007; Njome and De Wit, 2014 ). The Edea – Eseka region (EER; 3 � 25 0 –4 � 00 0
N and 10 � 00 0 –10 � 55 0 E) belong to the South Cameroon domain cross-cut by the Sanaga Fault (SF) and developed on a Proterozoic metamorphic basement and sedimentary formations ( Fig. 2 ). The crystallophyllian rocks have been abundantly explored by petrographical, geochemical, structural and geochronological studies conducting various models still under discussion ( Ne´de´lec et al., 1993; Lerouge et al., 2006; Nsangou Ngapna, 2017; Owona et al., 2020a, 2021a, 2021b; Moussango Ibohn et al., 2022 ). In the geo- morphological point of view, previous studies on the EER using geomorphometric approaches have established
the geological and climatic controls of its landscape ( Nsangou Ngapna et al., 2013, 2018, 2019, 2020 ). Interestingly, several studies have been done by using Analytical Hierarchy Processes (AHP) models to solve the geo-environmental problems ( Zhang et al., 2016; Sivakumar and Ghosh, 2021; Padder et al., 2022; Zangmene et al., 2023 ). Moreover, many geomorphic indices combinations were used to assess the relative tec- tonic activity index (IAT), representing the distribution of regional tectonic activity over a number of drainage basins (e.g. El Hamdouni et al., 2008; Mahmood and Gloaguen, 2012; Jaberi et al., 2018; Anand and Pradhan, 2019; Balla Ateba et al., 2023 ). Thus, the EER appears to be a good example for the assessment of the relative active tectonic in a SW Cameroon Atlantic Coast ( Nsangou Ngapna et al., 2020 ). Nevertheless, these EER results remain argu- able. For the same indices, Alipoor et al. (2011), Argyriou et al. (2017), Saber et al. (2020) and Al-Attar et al., 2022 considered the AHP, instead of the computation of the arithmetic mean: the degree and weight of each index was considered for the representation of the neotectonic activ- ity over each drainage basin. Therefore, an attempt to eval- uate the neotectonic variability using AHP method in EER is indicated. The EER present study focuses on the deter- mination of the AHP of 32 basins (differs from one pro- vince to another and within each province). To achieve this objective, we combine eleven morphometric parame- ters which are grouped into four categories (see Table 1 ) such as: watershed geometry (elongation ratio and basin shape), relief characteristics (relative relief ratio, slope watershed and hypsometric integral), drainage texture analysis (drainage density) and regional tilting/uplift (drai- nage basin asymmetry, normalized steepness index, stream- length gradient index, valley floor width to valley height ratio and transverse topographic symmetry factor), in order to classify the area into different neotectonic activity classes.
2. The study area overview
2.1. Geological setting
The EER belongs to the SW Cameroonian passive mar- gin which consists of the Paleoproterozoic Nyong and

Location map showing (a) the Africa landform surface; (b) the Cameroon landform surface dissected by its main tectonic features, (c) the Africa geologic sketch map showing the study area; (d) Cameroon geological sketch (Modified after Tchameni et al., 2001; Toteu et al., 2001; Owona et al., 2012 ); (e) Geological map of the SW Cameroon margin (adapted from Dumort, 1968; Maurizot et al., 1986 ) showing epicentres of recorded earthquakes in on– and offshore since 1900 to present (adopted from Ambraseys and Adams, 1986; Bekoa et al., 1992; Tabot et al., 1992; Ntepe et al., 2004; Eloumala Onana et al., 2014 ). Abbreviations: CS: Congo Shield; KS: Kalahari Shield; SFS: Sao Francisco Shield; TS: Tanzania Shield; WAS: West African Shield; AS: Amazonian shield; WAS: West African shield; SFC: Sao Francisco shield; LSS: Louis Sao Shield; WAOB: West African orogenic belt; WACOB: West African Central orogenic belt; EB: Eastern Bahia orogenic belt; MQF: Mineiro and Quadrila´tero Ferrı´fero orogenic belts; CCSZ: Central Cameroon shear zone; SF: Sanaga Fault; TBF: Tchollire´-Banyo Fault; SF: Sanaga Fault; BF: Boa Fault; EF: Ekona Fault; TF: Tiko Fault; DF: Dibamba Fault; NF: Nyong Fault; KF: Kribi Fault; CP: Coastal Plain; SCLP: South Cameroon Low Plateau; WCHL: West Cameroon Highlands; AHP: Adamawa High Plateau; BT: Benue Trough; CT: Chad Trough; CFZ: Cameroon Fracture Zone; FPFZ: Fernando Po Fracture Zone. White dashed box designates the study area.
